title: A Heart Cleansed by the Spirit
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: A Heart Cleansed by the Spirit - Pastor Tito Lyro - Matthew 5_8.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Already and Not Yet of the Beatitudes
subpoints: The pure in heart will see God — but who can come before Him with a pure heart?
All the beatitudes are already-and-not-yet statements
We are already pure in heart (positional); we are becoming pure in heart (progressive)
Psalm 24 asks who may ascend the hill of the Lord — only the one with clean hands and a pure heart

outline.title: We Are Already Pure in Heart
subpoints: The new covenant promise: God gives a new heart and puts a new spirit within (Ezekiel 36)
Regeneration by the Spirit is the fulfillment of this covenant promise
Jesus ascended to God's holy hill with clean hands — and we are in union with Him
We are washed, sanctified, and justified in the name of the Lord Jesus (1 Corinthians 6:9-11)
Our identity is no longer defined by our sins but by our union with Christ the new creation

outline.title: We Are Becoming Pure in Heart
subpoints: A new pure heart produces a pursuit of purity — you cannot pursue purity without first having a pure heart
God told Israel to circumcise the foreskin of their heart — God initiates, then we follow
We pursue purity through the ordinary means of grace
The pursuit of purity is a community project: we need each other

outline.title: How to Pursue Purity: The Ordinary Means of Grace
subpoints: Reading, hearing, memorizing, and meditating on the Word of God — Jesus sanctifies by the truth
Obeying what the Bible says — we purify our souls by obeying the truth (1 Peter 1:22-25)
Prayer — create in me a clean heart, O God (Psalm 51)
Being involved in each other's lives — restoring one another in a spirit of gentleness
Attending the sacraments — the Lord's Supper gives grace to resist temptation

outline.title: Conclusion: We Will See God
subpoints: We already see God with the eyes of faith — no less real for being unseen
One day we will see Him as He is, for we shall be like Him (1 John 3:1-2)
Purity of heart is not only cleanness but commitment of heart and will to the Lord
He has given us a pure heart; now we live in purity by His grace

summary:
In this Sunday morning sermon on Matthew 5:8 — "Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God" — Pastor Tito Lyro organizes the beatitude around the theological framework of the "already and not yet." Since Christ's resurrection and ascension, believers both already are pure in heart (positionally, by virtue of the new birth and union with Christ) and are becoming pure in heart (progressively, through sanctification by the Spirit). Both truths must be held together.

The "already" is established through the new covenant promise of Ezekiel 36, where God pledges to sprinkle clean water on His people and give them a new heart — a promise fulfilled in regeneration. Because of our union with Christ, the Father sees us as holy and pure, just as He sees the Son. Paul's list in 1 Corinthians 6:9-11 — adulterers, fornicators, homosexuals, covetous, drunkards, extortioners — is introduced with "such were some of you," but the identity of believers is now defined not by past sin but by the washing, sanctification, and justification accomplished in Christ.

The "not yet" concerns the progressive work of sanctification. Because believers have been given new hearts, they now pursue purity — and there is no true pursuit of purity without first having a pure heart. This pursuit is not achieved through extraordinary gifts or new revelation but through the ordinary means of grace: reading, hearing, memorizing, and obeying the Word; prayer; accountability within the body of Christ; and attending the sacraments. The sermon closes with the vision of 1 John 3:2 — "when He is revealed, we shall be like Him, for we shall see Him as He is" — as the ultimate fulfillment of this beatitude.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Matthew 5:1-12 (The Beatitudes)

For our scripture reading today, we're going to once again turn to Matthew chapter 5, starting at verse 1 and reading through verse 12. This is the passage commonly known as the Beatitudes, the beginning of the Sermon on the Mount. Here is the Word of our Lord.

"And seeing the multitudes, He went up on a mountain, and when He was seated His disciples came to Him. Then He opened His mouth and taught them, saying: Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted. Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be filled. Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness' sake, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when they revile and persecute you, and say all kinds of evil against you falsely for My sake. Rejoice and be exceedingly glad, for great is your reward in heaven, for so they persecuted the prophets who were before you."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father, we pray to bless the proclamation of your Word. We pray for your Spirit to be with the proclamation and with the hearing of your Word this morning. In Jesus' name, amen.

## Introduction: Who Is Able to See God?

Do you remember Juan Valdez from the coffee commercials — "100% Colombian coffee, the richest coffee in the world"? When I first came to the United States, I was very puzzled by this. I grew up in Colombia where Colombian coffee was the cheapest coffee you could get because it had no taste. So to me "100% pure Colombian coffee" meant 100% pure tasteless coffee. The idea that it was 100% pure was alluring, but it was just a marketing play.

What Jesus says here, however, is no marketing play. The happy person is the one who is pure in heart, because he or she will see God. This beatitude in verse 8 is a reflection of Psalm 24. All the beatitudes are reflections from different psalms, but this one is from Psalm 24:3-5: "Who may ascend into the hill of the Lord, or who may stand in His holy place? He who has clean hands and a pure heart, who has not lifted up his soul to an idol, nor sworn deceitfully. He shall receive blessing from the Lord, and righteousness from the God of his salvation."

Now if this is the case — if only those with clean hands and a pure heart can see God — who is able to see God? I know my own heart. Jeremiah 17:9: "The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked." Jesus Himself says in Matthew 15:19: "For out of the heart proceed evil thoughts, murders, adulteries, fornications, thefts, false witness, blasphemies." So who is able to see God?

To answer this, we must think of the beatitudes as "already and not yet" statements at the same time. Since the resurrection and ascension of our Lord Jesus, we live in a period when He has already established His kingdom, already defeated Satan, already delivered us from sin and death. But some aspects of His work and reign are not yet fully manifested and will not be until His coming again. Each beatitude includes both aspects — they tell us what we already are in Christ and what we are becoming through the work of the Spirit in sanctification.

## Already Pure in Heart

Let us start with the words themselves. **Blessed** — both in the Old and New Testament, this word means happy, full of joy. That is our identity in Christ. **Pure** — from the Greek word katharos, from which we get "catharsis." The idea is of cleansing from something impure, making pure what was once defiled. Jesus uses it in Matthew 23:26: "First cleanse the inside of the cup." In the upper room discourse He says to His disciples: "You are already clean because of the word I have spoken to you." **Heart** — the heart is who the person truly is when there is no pretense, no barriers, no masks. It is the person's nature, the wellspring of life. Proverbs 4:23: "Keep your heart with all diligence, for out of it spring the issues of life."

Now, what I propose is this: if you have placed your faith in Jesus Christ, **you are already pure in heart** because of the new covenant, because of the work of the Spirit in regeneration.

Remember the promise of the new covenant in Ezekiel 36:25-26: "Then I will sprinkle clean water on you, and you shall be clean; I will cleanse you from all your filthiness and from all your idols. I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you; I will take the heart of stone out of your flesh and give you a heart of flesh." This is the new birth — the regeneration Christ speaks about in John 3. The Spirit grabs people who are dead in their trespasses and sins and makes them alive through Jesus Christ.

Ephesians 2:1-7: "You He made alive who were dead in trespasses and sins... But God, who is rich in mercy, because of His great love with which He loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you have been saved — and raised us up together, and made us sit together in the heavenly places in Christ Jesus."

Psalm 24 describes Jesus as the righteous one who ascends before the Lord. This psalm has been historically associated with Christ's ascension in Acts 1. Jesus ascended to God's holy hill with clean hands and a pure heart. He is the one who never bowed to an idol. And because of our union with Him, the Father sees us as He sees Jesus — pure and holy.

Further, He has freed us from the power of sin. In 1 Corinthians 6:9-11, Paul lists: "Neither fornicators, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor homosexuals, nor sodomites, nor thieves, nor covetous, nor drunkards, nor revilers, nor extortioners will inherit the kingdom of God. **And such were some of you.** But you were washed, but you were sanctified, but you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus and by the Spirit of our God." This is who you were — "such were." Past tense. That is no longer your identity. You are not identified by your sins anymore; you are identified by your union with Christ. In 2 Corinthians 5:17: "If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; old things have passed away; behold, all things have become new."

Romans 6:11 and 14: "Likewise you also, reckon yourselves to be dead indeed to sin, but alive to God in Christ Jesus our Lord. For sin shall not have dominion over you, for you are not under law but under grace." Notice he says, "Reckon yourself, think of yourself, count yourself as dead to sin." It is already a reality. Sin does not have dominion over us anymore.

## Not Yet Pure in Heart: Becoming Pure

But there is also a "not yet" aspect. We are becoming pure in heart. One result of having a new pure heart is that we now pursue purity. Paul urges Timothy: "Pursue righteousness, faith, love, peace with those who call on the Lord out of a pure heart" (2 Timothy 2:22). You cannot pursue purity without first having a pure heart. You cannot truly seek Christ if your heart has not already been regenerated.

God told Israel in Deuteronomy 10:16: "Circumcise the foreskin of your heart and be stiff-necked no longer." And in 30:6: "The Lord your God will circumcise your heart and the heart of your descendants, to love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul, that you may live." We pursue purity because God first pursues us and gives us a pure heart. God initiates; then we follow.

## How to Pursue Purity: The Ordinary Means of Grace

How do I keep my heart pure and pursue purity? There is no fancy strategy here, no extraordinary gifting, no new revelation. We pursue purity by using the ordinary means of grace — the common things God gave us to grow in Him. And I know that is not appealing to us, because we want something extraordinary. But it is the everyday things that allow us to pursue purity.

**The Word of God.** Reading it, hearing it proclaimed, memorizing it, meditating on it. Jesus prays in John 17:17: "Sanctify them by Your truth. Your word is truth." Psalm 119:9-11: "How can a young man cleanse his ways? By taking heed according to Your word. Your word I have hidden in my heart, that I might not sin against You." Hebrews 4:12-13: "For the word of God is living and powerful, and sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing even to the division of soul and spirit, and of joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart. And there is no creature hidden from His sight."

**Obeying what the Bible says.** 1 Peter 1:22-25: "Since you have purified your souls in obeying the truth through the Spirit, in sincere love of the brethren, love one another fervently with a pure heart, having been born again, not of corruptible seed but incorruptible, through the word of God."

**Prayer.** The great prayer of David in Psalm 51: "Create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a steadfast spirit within me." Psalm 19:12-13: "Who can understand his errors? Cleanse me from secret faults. Keep back Your servant also from presumptuous sins."

**Being involved in each other's lives.** Galatians 6: "Brethren, if a man is overtaken in any trespass, you who are spiritual restore such a one in a spirit of gentleness, considering yourself lest you also be tempted." 2 Corinthians 7:1: "Therefore, having these promises, beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God." Notice "let us" — this is a community project; we need each other.

**Attending the sacraments.** We practice the Lord's Supper every week for good reason. 1 Corinthians 10:13-14: God gives grace for us to resist any temptation. Then Paul goes directly into a discussion of the Lord's Supper, saying: "The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ? For we, though many, are one bread and one body, for we all partake of the one bread." We grow together, able to resist sin, as by His grace we partake of the sacraments.

## Conclusion: We Will See God

So how do we pursue purity? We read the Bible, hear the Word proclaimed, memorize it, think about it, obey it, pray to the Lord, participate in each other's lives, and come together in the sacraments. Those are the ordinary means God has given us for us to grow in purity.

Purity of heart is not only a matter of cleanliness, but also a matter of commitment of the heart and will to the Lord. He has given you a pure heart. Now we live in purity by His grace.

1 John 3:1-2: "Behold, what manner of love the Father has bestowed on us, that we should be called children of God! Therefore the world does not know us, because it did not know Him. Beloved, now we are children of God; and it has not yet been revealed what we shall be, but we know that when He is revealed, we shall be like Him, for **we shall see Him as He is**." That is the fulfillment of this beatitude — the blessed vision of God that awaits every believer.

## Closing Prayer

Father, we thank you that the pure one, the Lord Jesus Christ, has gone before us. And we pray that as we follow Him with pure hearts — the hearts that you have given to us through the new birth — we would also become pure in practice, that we would avail ourselves of the very means that you have given to us to be pure, that we might follow Christ. In His name we pray, amen.
